figures.caption: Table 1: Training strategies with increasing derivative supervision. All methods include the data loss Ldata with unit weight. Non-zero entries indicate which derivative supervision terms are included.

Figure 1: Phase space trajectories of the Lorenz 63 system under the minimal temporal setting (m = 1). (a) Full true trajectory (t ∈ [0, 320]), (b) truncated true trajectory (t ∈ [20, 320]), and (c) learned trajectory of naive model.
Figure 2: Phase space trajectories of the learned models of (a) mc, (b) mcjac, (c) jac, and (d) hes under the minimal temporal setting (m = 1).

Table 2: Statistical comparison between the ground-truth and learned Lorenz 63 systems under the minimal temporal setting (m = 1). W 1 denotes the Wasserstein-1 distance. Here, λ̂j denotes the j-th Lyapunov exponent estimated from the learned NODE, while λref j denotes the corresponding reference exponent computed from the simulated Lorenz 63 trajectories using the same evaluation interval t ∈ [20, 320].

Figure 4: Robustness of largest Lyapunov exponent (λ1) estimation under a minimal rollout length (m = 1) for the Lorenz 63 system. (a) Full scale view shows significant outliers in mc and jac methods. (b) Zoomed-in view highlights that both mcjac and hes achieve more accurate and consistent estimates, closely matching the reference value (λref 1 = 0.9041, indicated by the red dashed line).

Figure 5: Phase space trajectories of the learned Lorenz 63 model, colored by time evolution (from purple to yellow). (a) The jac method captures the global attractor structure but collapses toward a spurious fixed point near the lobe-transition region. (b)–(c) The mc method initially follows the attractor but later exhibits orbit drift, failing to preserve long-term chaotic invariance.

Figure 6: Hessian Frobenius norm ∥Ĥ∥F of each learned model evaluated along Lorenz 63 trajectories at m = 1: (a) mc, (b) mcjac, (c) jac, (d) hes. The colormap ranges from 1 (black) to 20 (yellow); values above 20 are clipped. The true Lorenz 63 vector field is bilinear, so its Hessian is spatially constant (∥H∥F = 2, nearly black) at every point of phase space. Mean (std) across the trajectory is 6.76 (3.97), 2.06 (0.17), 3.43 (2.35), 2.01 (0.08), respectively (Table 3).
Table 4: Statistical comparison as in Table 2, except for the m = 2 setting.

Figure 7: Impact of increasing rollout length (m = 2) on λ1 estimation for the Lorenz 63 system. (a) Full scale view demonstrates a reduction in outliers compared to m = 1, with most methods (except naive) converging near the true value. (b) Zoomed-in view reveals that mcjac and hes are accurate and exhibit lower variance.

Figure 8: Hovmöller diagrams of the slow variables Xk for t ∈ [0, 10] at F = 10. Predicted trajectories from each learned model (left) and differences from the true trajectory (right) are shown. All five methods reproduce the characteristic wave-like spatiotemporal structures of the system with comparable short-term accuracy. The second-order supervised methods (mcjac and hes) also exhibit slightly smaller short-time residuals during the early-time interval (t ≤ 2).

Figure 9: Hovmöller diagrams of the slow variables Xk for t ∈ [0, 10] at F = 20. All methods retain similar spatiotemporal structures. However, the naive model exhibits stronger localized high-amplitude regions.

Figure 10: Long-horizon Hovmöller diagrams of the slow variables Xk for t ∈ [0, 500] at F = 20. mcjac and hes show very good agreement with the true dynamics over the entire rollout. The naive model remains bounded but shows intermittent localized highamplitude bursts. The mc model transitions near t ≈ 70 into a spurious high-amplitude attractor-like regime for most of the rollout. The jac model similarly enters a spurious regime near t ≈ 80, briefly recovers over t ≈ 350–430, and later transitions back.

Figure 11: Top-20 Lyapunov spectra of the learned models. (a) At F = 10, all methods remain in good agreement with the true spectrum. (b) At F = 20, clear separation emerges. jac, mcjac and hes remain closer to the true spectrum across the leading modes, while mc moderately overestimates and naive strongly overestimates the positive Lyapunov exponents.

Figure 12: Distributions of the curvature length scale Lcurv on the trajectory. (a) At F = 10, all methods produce nearly identical single-peaked distributions centered near Lcurv ≈ 0.263. (b) At F = 20, mcjac and hes remain centered near the true peak at Lcurv ≈ 0.369, while naive remains single-peaked but shifted to approximately 0.415. In contrast, mc and jac become bimodal, with a small peak near the true value and a broader secondary lobe spanning approximately 0.7–1.4, indicating predominantly transitions into regions with distorted local nonlinear geometry.

Figure 13: Marginal densities of X at four representative sites (X0, X9, X18, and X27). (a) At F = 10, all methods overlap closely with the true density (black). (b) At F = 20, mcjac and hes remain in close agreement with the true density, mc and jac produce broadened marginals with heavy tails, and naive remains comparatively close to the true density at these representative sites.

Figure 14: Jacobian recovery at (a) F = 10 and (b) F = 20. Scatter plots of the learned versus true Jacobian spectral radius (top) and histograms of the Frobenius-norm Jacobian error (bottom) are shown. At F = 10, all methods are broadly aligned with the diagonal, but the Frobenius means differ by nearly two orders of magnitude (0.03 for hes to 1.91 for naive). At F = 20, hes and mcjac remain closest to the diagonal, while mc, jac, and especially naive show increasingly distorted Jacobian spectra. In the Frobeniuserror distributions, hes is most concentrated near zero, mcjac retains a strong low-error peak with a broader secondary component, mc and jac shift to broad intermediate-error distributions, and naive has the largest errors overall.

Figure 15: Site-averaged Wasserstein-1 distance between learned and true marginal distributions of Xk as a function of forcing F . The inset shows the mild regime F ∈ [10, 16]. For F ≤ 16 all five methods produce W 1 ≲ 0.4 and remain essentially indistinguishable. Between F = 16 and F = 18, mc and jac undergo an abrupt increase of one to two orders of magnitude, naive degrades moderately, and mcjac and hes remain close to the true marginals across the entire sweep.

Table 6: MSE of the top-20 Lyapunov exponents and training wall-clock time for each method across forcing F . The errors are evaluated at F ∈ {10, 16, 18, 20}, while wall-clock times are reported in seconds.
Table A.8: Hyperparameters for the coupled Lorenz 96 experiments (m = 2).
Table A.7: Hyperparameters for the Lorenz 63 experiments (m = 1, 2).
